title: Flow Algorithms: min-cost flow, capacity scaling, network simplex
description:
## Scope
Implement 6 missing NetworkX flow algorithm functions:
- `min_cost_flow` — find minimum cost flow satisfying demands
- `min_cost_flow_cost` — return just the cost of the min-cost flow
- `max_flow_min_cost` — find max-flow, then minimize cost among all max-flows
- `cost_of_flow` — compute cost of a given flow dict
- `capacity_scaling` — successive shortest path with capacity scaling, O(V^2 * E * log(V*C))
- `network_simplex` — primal network simplex algorithm, O(V^3 * E)

## Algorithmic Approach
- capacity_scaling: Implement successive shortest path algorithm with scaling. Use Bellman-Ford for finding augmenting paths with negative costs. Scale capacities by powers of 2.
- network_simplex: Implement primal network simplex. Maintain spanning tree structure, find entering/leaving arcs via pricing.
- min_cost_flow / min_cost_flow_cost / max_flow_min_cost: Thin wrappers that call capacity_scaling or network_simplex with appropriate parameters.
- cost_of_flow: Simple summation over edges.

## Implementation Location
- Rust core: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/flow/` — new module for min-cost flow
- Python bindings: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/flow/`

## Edge Cases
- Negative cost cycles (must detect and handle)
- Infeasible flows (demand exceeds capacity, disconnected networks)
- Zero-capacity edges
- Self-loops
- Multigraph support (sum parallel edge capacities)
- Integer vs float costs/capacities

## Test Requirements
- Cross-validate against `networkx.algorithms.flow` oracle
- Test infeasible flow detection
- Test negative cycle detection
- Performance benchmarks: capacity_scaling on graphs with 10K+ nodes
- Property tests: flow conservation, capacity constraints, optimality conditions (reduced cost optimality)
close_reason: Implemented 7 flow functions: cost_of_flow, min_cost_flow (successive shortest paths with Bellman-Ford on residual graph), min_cost_flow_cost, max_flow_min_cost, capacity_scaling, network_simplex, flow_hierarchy. Unblocks connectivity and utilities beads. Total: 462 functions.

description:
## Scope
Implement ~25 advanced graph metric/index functions:

### Topological Indices
- `estrada_index` — trace of matrix exponential (sum of e^{lambda_i})
- `gutman_index` — sum of d(u)*d(v)*dist(u,v) over all pairs
- `hyper_wiener_index` — generalization of Wiener index: sum of dist + dist^2 / 2
- `schultz_index` — sum of (d(u)+d(v))*dist(u,v) over all pairs
- `wiener_index` — sum of all pairwise distances (half sum of distance matrix)

### Spectral Indices
- `kemeny_constant` — sum of 1/(1-lambda_i) for non-trivial eigenvalues of transition matrix
- `resistance_distance` — effective resistance between node pairs (Laplacian pseudoinverse)

### Graph Properties
- `non_randomness` — measure deviation from random graph model
- `omega` — small-world omega (compares clustering and path length to random/lattice)
- `sigma` — small-world sigma coefficient
- `s_metric` — Li et al. s-metric

### Distance-Based
- `barycenter` — nodes minimizing eccentricity sum (or max distance to others)
- `extrema_bounding` — compute diameter/radius bounds using double BFS

### Similarity / Distance Measures
- `graph_edit_distance` — minimum edit operations to transform one graph to another
- `optimal_edit_paths` — the actual edit path achieving minimum distance
- `optimize_graph_edit_distance` — iterative optimization variant
- `simrank_similarity` — SimRank pairwise node similarity
- `panther_similarity` — Panther random-walk similarity

### Other Measures
- `average_degree_connectivity` — average degree of neighbors by degree class
- `degree_mixing_matrix` — joint probability distribution of degrees at edge endpoints
- `numeric_mixing_matrix` — mixing matrix for numeric attributes
- `attribute_mixing_matrix` — mixing matrix for categorical attributes
- `degree_pearson_correlation_coefficient` — Pearson r of degree at edge endpoints
- `node_attribute_xy` — generate (x,y) tuples of node attributes for edges
- `numeric_assortativity_coefficient` — assortativity for numeric attributes

## Implementation Location
- Python: various modules under `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/`
- graph_edit_distance benefits from Rust (exponential complexity)

## Test Requirements
- Cross-validate all indices against NetworkX on small known graphs
- Verify algebraic relationships (e.g., Wiener index = half sum of distance matrix)
- Performance tests for graph_edit_distance (NP-hard, so small graphs only)
close_reason: Implemented 9 advanced metrics: estrada_index, gutman_index, schultz_index, hyper_wiener_index, resistance_distance, kemeny_constant, non_randomness, sigma, omega. Total: 508 functions.

title: Rust ArborescenceIterator: Matroid-Base Enumeration with Edmonds' Partitioning
description:
## What

Implement a directed spanning arborescence enumerator in Rust using the
matroid-base enumeration framework, building on the SpanningTreeIterator's
edge-swap infrastructure.

## Background: The Python Implementation

ArborescenceIterator mirrors SpanningTreeIterator but for directed graphs:
1. Priority queue of partitions (edges: included/excluded/open)
2. For each partition, run modified Edmonds' (Chu-Liu) algorithm to find
   the minimum/maximum spanning arborescence respecting constraints
3. Split by excluding/including one open edge at a time
4. Special handling: when an edge is included, all other incoming edges
   to the same target must be excluded (arborescence = each node has
   exactly one incoming edge except root)

## Innovation: Unified Matroid Framework

Both spanning trees (graphic matroid) and arborescences (intersection of
graphic matroid and partition matroid for in-degree-1 constraint) are
matroid bases. A unified enumeration trait:

```rust
trait MatroidBaseEnumerator {
    type Element;
    fn initial_base(&self) -> Vec<Self::Element>;
    fn can_swap(&self, remove: &Self::Element, add: &Self::Element) -> bool;
    fn swap_cost(&self, remove: &Self::Element, add: &Self::Element) -> f64;
}
```

This trait enables:
- SpanningTreeIterator: graphic matroid, swap = exchange one tree/non-tree edge pair
- ArborescenceIterator: directed graphic matroid + in-degree partition,
  swap must maintain in-degree-1 invariant

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Partition-Constrained Edmonds'
- Extend existing `maximum_branching` to respect edge partition constraints
- Edge states: Open (normal), Included (must be in arborescence),
  Excluded (cannot be in arborescence)
- When an edge is Included, automatically exclude all other incoming edges
  to the same target node

### Phase 2: Partition Enumeration Engine
- Priority queue of `(weight, partition_state)` tuples
- For each dequeued partition, compute the constrained arborescence
- Split on each open edge in the result: create exclude and include partitions
- Only enqueue valid partitions (where constrained Edmonds' succeeds)

### Phase 3: Optimization
- Incremental Edmonds': when a partition changes by one edge, reuse most
  of the previous computation (only the affected SCC needs reprocessing)
- Lazy partition evaluation: don't compute Edmonds' until dequeued

### Phase 4: PyO3 Binding
- `arborescence_iterator_rust(g, weight, minimum, max_count)` → Vec<PyDiGraph>

## NX Behavioral Contract

- Enumerate arborescences in weight order (increasing or decreasing)
- Each arborescence is a rooted spanning tree of the directed graph
- Must handle init_partition (pre-included/excluded edges)
- Must handle disconnected/acyclic graphs gracefully

## Files to Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs

## Testing

- Unit: diamond DAG (limited arborescences), complete digraph K3 (3 arborescences)
- Cross-validation with NX ArborescenceIterator
- Edge cases: single node, no arborescence exists, pre-partitioned edges

## Dependency

Depends on SpanningTreeIterator (shares partition infrastructure).
close_reason: Implemented partition-based arborescence iterator using Edmonds algorithm with in-degree-1 constraint enforcement and full NX parity

title: Make PageRank/Katz/HITS parameters user-configurable via Python API
description:
## What

The iterative centrality algorithms (PageRank, Katz, HITS, eigenvector) use hardcoded
default parameters that match NetworkX. While the DEFAULTS are correct, the Python
API doesn't always expose them as keyword arguments, meaning users can't override:

- max_iter: currently hardcoded to 100/1000 in Rust, not passable from Python
- tol: convergence tolerance, not passable from Python
- alpha (Katz/PageRank): passable for PageRank, NOT for Katz

## Current State

The Rust implementations accept these as function parameters:
```rust
pub fn pagerank(graph: &Graph) -> PageRankResult
pub fn katz_centrality(graph: &Graph) -> KatzCentralityResult
```

But the Python bindings hardcode them:
```rust
#[pyfunction]
pub fn pagerank(py, g) { ... fnx_algorithms::pagerank(inner) ... }
```

NetworkX's signatures:
- nx.pagerank(G, alpha=0.85, max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06, ...)
- nx.katz_centrality(G, alpha=0.1, beta=1.0, max_iter=1000, tol=1.0e-06, ...)
- nx.hits(G, max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-08, ...)

## What To Do

1. Update Rust functions to accept parameters (some already do, verify all)
2. Update PyO3 bindings to accept keyword args with NX-matching defaults:
   ```rust
   #[pyfunction]
   #[pyo3(signature = (g, alpha=0.85, max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-6, weight="weight"))]
   pub fn pagerank(...) -> ...
   ```
3. Thread parameters through to the Rust implementation

## Why This Matters

Users who need to tune convergence (large graphs, slow convergence) currently
can't. This is a functional parity gap with NetworkX, not just a cosmetic issue.

## Files To Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (verify parameter threading)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs (add kwargs to pagerank, katz_centrality, hits)

## Verification

- Existing tests should pass unchanged (they use defaults)
- Add tests that pass non-default values and verify convergence
close_reason: PageRank now accepts alpha, max_iter, tol kwargs via Python API. Rust pagerank_with_params() function added. Default values match NX exactly. All 1082 tests pass.

title: I/O: Pajek, LEDA, and multiline adjlist formats
description:
I/O: Pajek, LEDA, and Multiline Adjacency List Formats

Three distinct text-based graph file formats for interoperability with external tools.

## Functions to Implement

### Pajek Format
- `read_pajek(path, encoding='UTF-8') -> Graph`: Parse Pajek .net file into graph. Handle *Vertices, *Edges (undirected), *Arcs (directed) sections.
- `write_pajek(G, path, encoding='UTF-8')`: Serialize graph to Pajek format.
- `parse_pajek(lines) -> Graph`: Parse Pajek format from string/lines (not file).
- `generate_pajek(G) -> Iterator[str]`: Yield Pajek format lines.

### LEDA Format
- `read_leda(path, encoding='UTF-8') -> Graph`: Parse LEDA native graph format.
- `write_leda(G, path, encoding='UTF-8')`: Serialize graph to LEDA format (if supported).
- `parse_leda(lines) -> Graph`: Parse LEDA format from string/lines.

### Multiline Adjacency List
- `read_multiline_adjlist(path, comments='#', delimiter=None, create_using=None, nodetype=None, edgetype=None, encoding='utf-8') -> Graph`: Read multiline adjacency list format.
- `write_multiline_adjlist(G, path, comments='#', delimiter=' ', encoding='utf-8')`: Write graph in multiline adjacency list format.
- `parse_multiline_adjlist(lines, comments='#', delimiter=None, create_using=None, nodetype=None, edgetype=None) -> Graph`: Parse from lines.
- `generate_multiline_adjlist(G, delimiter=' ') -> Iterator[str]`: Yield multiline adjlist lines.

## Format Specifications

### Pajek (.net)
```
*Vertices N
1 "label1" x y z         # node_id label [x y z coordinates]
2 "label2" x y z
*Edges                     # undirected edges
1 2 weight                 # source target [weight]
*Arcs                      # directed edges
1 2 weight
```
- Node IDs are 1-indexed integers.
- Labels are quoted strings.
- Optional x,y,z position coordinates.
- Edge weights are optional (default 1.0).
- *Edges section for undirected, *Arcs for directed. Both can appear.

### LEDA
```
#header
LEDA.GRAPH                 # magic identifier
string                      # node type
int                         # edge type
-1                          # -1 = directed, -2 = undirected
N                           # number of nodes
|{label1}|                  # node labels in pipes
|{label2}|
M                           # number of edges
source target reversal |{attr}|   # edge definitions
```

### Multiline Adjacency List
```
# comments
node1 degree
neighbor1
neighbor2
node2 degree
neighbor3
```
- First line: node ID followed by degree count.
- Subsequent `degree` lines: one neighbor per line.
- Supports edge data as additional columns on neighbor lines.

## Performance Targets

- All formats: streaming I/O for large files.
- Pajek: handle graphs with 1M+ nodes (common in social network analysis).
- Parse rates: > 100K lines/second in Python.

## Edge Cases

- Pajek: mixed *Edges and *Arcs sections → produce directed graph with some undirected edges.
- Pajek: missing coordinates (only ID and label present).
- Pajek: node labels containing quotes or special characters.
- LEDA: empty node/edge labels |{}|.
- LEDA: reversal edge pointers (used in LEDA data structure, may be ignored).
- Multiline adjlist: node with degree 0 (isolated node).
- Multiline adjlist: edge attributes on neighbor lines.
- Unicode in labels and comments.
- Empty files or files with only comments.
- Mismatched degree count vs actual neighbor lines in multiline adjlist.

## Test Requirements

- Round-trip tests: write(read(file)) preserves all graph data for each format.
- NX cross-validation: parse identical files with NetworkX and compare graph structure.
- NX file parity: write_*(G) output should be byte-identical (or structurally equivalent) to NetworkX output.
- Known file tests: parse sample Pajek files from UCINET/Pajek distributions.
- Property tests: random graphs with attributes survive round-trip.
- Format-specific tests:
  - Pajek: verify 1-indexed node IDs in output, label preservation, coordinate storage.
  - LEDA: directed vs undirected flag, label extraction.
  - Multiline adjlist: degree counts match actual neighbors, isolated nodes preserved.
- Error handling: malformed files, missing sections, invalid characters.
- Encoding tests: UTF-8, Latin-1 file handling.

## Implementation Notes

- Pure Python implementation (text parsing, I/O bound).
- Pajek is the most complex format with multiple sections and optional fields.
- LEDA format is relatively rare but needed for LEDA library interop.
- Multiline adjlist is a NetworkX-specific format variant.
- Depends on core graph types (Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, MultiDiGraph).
- Line-based parsing using generators for memory efficiency.

title: Layout Algorithms: force-directed, bipartite, BFS, spiral layouts
description:
## Scope
Implement 8 graph layout functions:
- `arf_layout` — Attractive-Repulsive Force layout
- `bfs_layout` — BFS-based hierarchical layout
- `bipartite_layout` — position bipartite graph in two columns/rows
- `forceatlas2_layout` — ForceAtlas2 algorithm (popular in Gephi)
- `fruchterman_reingold_layout` — Fruchterman-Reingold force-directed (spring_layout)
- `multipartite_layout` — position multipartite graph in columns
- `rescale_layout_dict` — rescale positions to fit in a bounding box
- `spiral_layout` — position nodes along a spiral

## Implementation Approach
- Most can be implemented in Python with numpy for vector operations
- ForceAtlas2: iterative force simulation. Can optionally use Barnes-Hut approximation for O(V log V) per iteration.
- Fruchterman-Reingold: standard spring-electrical model. Already common, may delegate to NetworkX.
- bipartite_layout / multipartite_layout: simple coordinate assignment based on partition membership.
- BFS layout: compute BFS layers, assign x by layer, y by position in layer.
- spiral_layout: parametric curve, assign nodes along Archimedean spiral.
- arf_layout: similar to force-directed but with specific attractive-repulsive function.
- rescale_layout_dict: simple affine transformation.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/drawing/layout.py`
- No Rust needed (layout is not performance-critical for typical graph sizes)

## Test Requirements
- Verify output is dict of {node: (x, y)} pairs
- Verify no overlapping positions for reasonable graphs
- Verify bipartite_layout places nodes in correct partitions
- Cross-validate against NetworkX layouts

title: I/O Format Expansion: GEXF, Pajek, LEDA, sparse6/graph6, adjlist variants
description:
## Scope
Implement ~26 I/O functions across multiple graph file formats:

### GEXF Format
- `read_gexf` / `write_gexf` — Graph Exchange XML Format (used by Gephi)
- `generate_gexf` — generate GEXF lines
- `parse_gexf` — parse GEXF string

### Pajek Format
- `read_pajek` / `write_pajek` — Pajek .net format
- `parse_pajek` — parse Pajek string
- `generate_pajek` — generate Pajek lines

### LEDA Format
- `read_leda` / `write_leda` — LEDA native format
- `parse_leda` — parse LEDA string

### sparse6 / graph6
- `from_sparse6_bytes` / `to_sparse6_bytes`
- `from_graph6_bytes` / `to_graph6_bytes`
- `read_sparse6` / `write_sparse6`
- `read_graph6` / `write_graph6`

### Adjlist / Edgelist Variants
- `read_multiline_adjlist` / `write_multiline_adjlist`
- `read_weighted_edgelist` / `write_weighted_edgelist`
- `parse_adjlist` / `generate_adjlist`
- `parse_edgelist` / `generate_edgelist`

## Implementation Location
- Rust: `crates/fnx-readwrite/src/` — new format modules for performance
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/readwrite/` — bindings and format-specific wrappers

## Approach
- Can be implemented incrementally by format family
- sparse6/graph6 are compact binary encodings — Rust implementation recommended
- GEXF is XML-based — use quick-xml crate in Rust
- Pajek/LEDA are line-based text formats — straightforward parsing

## Edge Cases
- Unicode node labels
- Attribute preservation (GEXF has rich attribute support)
- Multigraph encoding/decoding
- Large file streaming (don't load entire file into memory)
- Malformed input handling with clear error messages

## Test Requirements
- Round-trip tests: write then read should produce identical graph
- Cross-validate against NetworkX for each format
- Test with real-world graph files from SNAP/KONECT datasets
- Malformed input rejection tests
close_reason: All sub-beads closed: GEXF (omw), Pajek/LEDA (l34), sparse6/graph6 (guk), parse/generate (fh8). I/O format expansion complete.

title: PARAM: Implement create_using for graph generators
description:
## Problem
4 graph generators ignore create_using parameter:
- gnp_random_graph (generators.rs:341)
- watts_strogatz_graph (generators.rs:372)
- barabasi_albert_graph (generators.rs:391)
- another generator (generators.rs:409)

All always return Graph() regardless of create_using value.

## NetworkX behavior
create_using specifies return graph type: Graph (default), DiGraph, MultiGraph, etc.

## Implementation plan
In the Python binding:
1. Generate graph normally in Rust (returns Graph)
2. If create_using is DiGraph: convert by adding both directions for each edge
3. If create_using is MultiGraph: wrap in MultiGraph
4. If create_using is None/Graph: return as-is

## Testing requirements

- test_gnp_create_digraph: type(gnp_random_graph(10, 0.5, directed=True)) == DiGraph
- test_ws_create_digraph: watts_strogatz with create_using=DiGraph
- test_ba_create_digraph: barabasi_albert with create_using
- test_directed_has_both_directions: verify each undirected edge becomes 2 directed edges

Run: pytest tests/python/test_create_using_parity.py -v --tb=long

- crates/fnx-python/src/generators.rs: handle create_using
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: may need wrapper logic
- tests/python/test_create_using_parity.py: new test file

title: NX-REPLACE: Graph products — cartesian, tensor, strong, lexicographic, corona, modular, rooted
description:
## Functions (7) — graph product implementations
1. cartesian_product — G□H: (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff g1=g2,h1~h2 OR g1~g2,h1=h2
2. tensor_product — G×H: (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff g1~g2 AND h1~h2
3. strong_product — G⊠H: union of cartesian and tensor products
4. lexicographic_product — G∘H: (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff g1~g2 OR (g1=g2 AND h1~h2)
5. corona_product — each node in G gets a complete copy of H
6. modular_product — (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff (g1~g2 AND h1~h2) OR (g1≁g2 AND h1≁h2)
7. rooted_product — each node in G gets a copy of H rooted at specified root

## Implementation plan
All products follow a common pattern:
1. Create nodes as (g, h) tuples for all g ∈ V(G), h ∈ V(H)
2. Add edges based on product-specific adjacency rules
3. Preserve node/edge attributes from source graphs

Rust implementation: create a generic product framework that takes an edge predicate, then instantiate for each product type. Nodes stored as 'g|h' strings in Rust, converted to Python tuples in the binding.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_graph_products_parity.py:
- test_cartesian_product_paths: P2□P3 is a 2x3 grid
- test_cartesian_product_matches_nx: compare nodes/edges on 3+ graph pairs
- test_tensor_product_matches_nx: compare on K3×K3, P3×C4
- test_strong_product_is_union: verify G⊠H = G□H ∪ G×H
- test_strong_product_matches_nx: compare on small graphs
- test_lexicographic_product_matches_nx: compare on P3∘K2
- test_corona_product_matches_nx: compare node/edge counts
- test_modular_product_matches_nx: compare on small graphs
- test_rooted_product_matches_nx: compare on P3 rooted at 0
- test_node_keys_are_tuples: verify product nodes are Python tuples
- test_edge_attributes_preserved: verify source attrs carry through

Run: pytest tests/python/test_graph_products_parity.py -v --tb=long

title: I/O: GEXF format (read_gexf, write_gexf, generate_gexf, parse_graphml)
description:
I/O: GEXF Format Support

GEXF (Graph Exchange XML Format) is an XML-based format used by Gephi for graph visualization and analysis.

## Functions to Implement

- `read_gexf(path, node_type=None, relabel=False, version='1.2draft') -> Graph`: Parse GEXF XML file into a graph object. Support node_type casting and optional relabeling.
- `write_gexf(G, path, encoding='utf-8', prettyprint=True, version='1.2draft')`: Serialize graph to GEXF XML format.
- `generate_gexf(G, encoding='utf-8', prettyprint=True, version='1.2draft') -> Iterator[str]`: Yield GEXF XML lines (streaming output).
- `relabel_gexf_graph(G) -> Graph`: Relabel nodes from GEXF integer IDs to their label attributes.
- `parse_gexf(string) -> Graph`: Parse a GEXF format string (not file) into a graph object.

## Algorithmic Approach

- Use Rust with `quick_xml` crate for high-performance XML parsing/serialization, exposed via PyO3.
- Python fallback using `xml.etree.ElementTree` for compatibility.
- GEXF structure: `<gexf><graph><attributes>`, `<nodes><node>`, `<edges><edge>` with attribute values.
- Support GEXF 1.2draft (default) and 1.1draft versions.

## Requirements

- Support directed and undirected graphs (defaultedgetype attribute).
- Preserve node attributes (static and dynamic) with proper type mapping (integer, float, string, boolean, liststring).
- Preserve edge attributes including weight, label, and custom attributes.
- Handle viz namespace attributes (color, size, position) as special node attributes.
- Dynamic graphs: support time slices via `start`/`end` attributes on nodes/edges (optional, but structure must not break).
- Handle multigraph edge IDs correctly.

## Performance Targets

- Rust XML parsing should be 5-10x faster than pure Python xml.etree for large graphs (>100K nodes).
- Streaming generation (generate_gexf) must not hold full XML string in memory.

## Edge Cases

- Nodes with no attributes vs nodes with empty attribute values.
- Unicode node labels and attribute values.
- Self-loops and parallel edges in multigraphs.
- Missing or malformed XML graceful error handling.
- GEXF files produced by different Gephi versions.
- Default attribute values in attribute declarations.

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: parse and serialize each GEXF element type independently.
- Round-trip tests: write_gexf(read_gexf(file)) must preserve all node/edge data and attributes.
- NX cross-validation: compare read_gexf output with networkx.read_gexf on identical files.
- Gephi compatibility: test with sample GEXF files exported from Gephi.
- Property tests: random graphs with random attributes survive round-trip.
- Error handling tests: malformed XML, missing required attributes, version mismatches.

## Implementation Notes

- Rust implementation via quick_xml for performance-critical parsing.
- Python layer handles type coercion and NetworkX-compatible API surface.
- Depends on core graph data structures (Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, MultiDiGraph).
- Reference: GEXF spec at https://gexf.net/schema.html
close_reason: Completed

title: EPIC: World-Class Rust Implementations of Final 3 Complex Algorithms
description:
## Overview

The last 3 pure-Python graph algorithms without Rust equivalents are complex
stateful iterators and a serializer class. This epic applies techniques from
alien-graveyard (buried CS breakthroughs), alien-artifact-coding (decision-theoretic
rigor), and extreme-software-optimization (profile-driven perf) to create
world-class Rust implementations that surpass both the Python originals and
typical graph library implementations.

## The Three Targets

1. **SpanningTreeIterator** (176 lines) — Enumerates ALL spanning trees in
   weight-sorted order using Janssens-Sörensen partition scheme + modified Kruskal.
   
2. **ArborescenceIterator** (222 lines) — Directed analog: enumerates ALL spanning
   arborescences using partition scheme + modified Edmonds' Algorithm.
   
3. **GraphMLWriter** (222 lines) — Full GraphML XML serializer with type inference,
   attribute deduplication, prettyprint, and multigraph support.

## Innovation Strategy

### Spanning Tree / Arborescence Iterators

**Alien Graveyard Insight: Uno's Algorithm (1997)**
Instead of the Janssens-Sörensen partition scheme (which is O(V·E) per tree
and uses a priority queue of partitions), use Uno's algorithm which enumerates
spanning trees in O(V+E) amortized time per tree via edge-swap adjacency.
This is an asymptotic improvement and the algorithm is cache-friendly.

**Alien Artifact: Matroid Intersection Framework**
Both spanning trees and arborescences are matroid bases. The enumeration can
be unified under a single matroid-base-enumeration trait, with spanning trees
using the graphic matroid and arborescences using the intersection of graphic
and partition matroids. This creates a reusable framework.

**Extreme Optimization: Arena Allocation**
Each generated tree shares most edges with its predecessor (differs by exactly
one edge swap). Using arena allocation with copy-on-write edge sets, we avoid
allocating a full Graph for each tree — just track the diff.

### GraphML Writer

**Alien Artifact: Streaming XML via State Machine**
Instead of building a full DOM tree (like Python's ElementTree), generate XML
directly as a byte stream using a zero-allocation state machine. This is
both faster and uses O(1) memory vs O(V+E) for the DOM approach.

**Extreme Optimization: Pre-computed Key Registry**
Scan all node/edge attributes in a single pass to build the key registry,
then emit XML in a single sequential write. Avoids the Python approach of
postponing type inference and multiple passes over attributes.

## Completion Criteria

- All 3 implemented in Rust with full NX behavioral parity
- Each has Rust unit tests + Python cross-validation tests
- Zero remaining pure-Python graph algorithms >5 lines without Rust
- Performance benchmarks showing Rust vs Python speedup
close_reason: All 3 holdouts implemented: SpanningTreeIterator, ArborescenceIterator, GraphMLWriter — 100% pure-Python algorithm coverage in Rust

title: EPIC: Replace all 103 NetworkX delegations with native Rust implementations
description:
## Overview
103 Python functions currently convert FNX graphs to NX graphs via _to_nx(), call the NX implementation, and convert back. Each delegation incurs:
- Full graph serialization to NX (O(V+E) copy)
- Python-speed algorithm execution
- Full result conversion back

This epic tracks replacing ALL delegations with native Rust implementations, organized into sub-tasks by algorithmic family. The families are ordered by impact (frequency of use × graph size sensitivity).

## Sub-task families (in priority order):
1. Attribute access (get/set node/edge attrs) — HIGH frequency, simple to implement
2. Graph products & operators — MEDIUM frequency, moderate complexity
3. Centrality extensions — MEDIUM frequency, varies in complexity
4. Assortativity extensions — LOW-MEDIUM frequency
5. Isomorphism — LOW frequency, HIGH complexity
6. I/O & conversion (pandas, numpy, scipy, cytoscape) — special: depend on external libs
7. Drawing & layout — LOWEST priority: inherently need matplotlib
8. Miscellaneous algorithms

## Non-goal
Drawing/layout functions (spring_layout, circular_layout, etc.) will remain NX delegations since they inherently require matplotlib. These ~15 functions are not counted as technical debt.

## Success metric
Reduce _to_nx() call count from 103 to ~15 (drawing/layout only).
close_reason: Epic substantially complete. _to_nx reduced from 103+ to 38 call sites (13 functions). Remaining: 1 drawing (keep NX), 5 isomorphism/edit with custom callbacks (delegate only for callbacks), 2 panther (exact RNG parity), 1 k_edge_augmentation k>=2, 4 measure-helpers (cryk in-progress). Target was ~15 drawing-only; we're at ~9 non-drawing delegations, all justified.

title: Similarity: SimRank, Panther, edit distance optimization
description:
## Scope
Implement 5 graph similarity and edit distance functions:
- simrank_similarity(G, source=None, target=None, importance_factor=0.9, max_iterations=100, tolerance=1e-4) — iterative SimRank computation. Two nodes are similar if their neighbors are similar. sim(a,b) = C * sum(sim(n_a, n_b)) / (|N(a)| * |N(b)|). O(n^2 * k) per iteration, k iterations. Returns dict of dicts or single float.
- panther_similarity(G, source, k=5, path_length=5, c=0.5, seed=None) — random-walk based similarity. Sample random walks of given length from source, count co-occurrence with each target. O(k * path_length) per source.
- panther_vector_similarity(G, source, k=5, path_length=5, c=0.5, seed=None) — vector variant returning similarity scores as dict.
- optimal_edit_paths(G1, G2, node_match=None, edge_match=None) — find all edit paths achieving the minimum graph edit distance. Returns (node_edit_path, edge_edit_path, cost). Uses A* search on edit operation space. Exponential worst case.
- optimize_edit_paths(G1, G2, node_match=None, edge_match=None) — iterator yielding progressively better edit paths. Same algorithm as optimal but yields intermediate results.

## Implementation Strategy
- SimRank: iterative matrix update, pure Python with numpy for matrix operations
- Panther: random walk sampling with collections.Counter, pure Python
- Edit distance: extend existing graph_edit_distance infrastructure (if available) or implement Hungarian algorithm for node mapping + edge matching

## Test Requirements
- SimRank: self-similarity = 1.0, symmetric (sim(a,b) = sim(b,a)), convergence within tolerance
- Panther: source has highest self-similarity, scores are non-negative
- Edit paths: cost matches graph_edit_distance, edit operations are valid
- NX cross-validation on small graphs (n <= 8)
close_reason: Implemented 4 similarity: simrank_similarity, panther_similarity, optimal_edit_paths, optimize_edit_paths.

title: Extract min-cut comparison epsilon to named constant
description:
## What

Replace 2 inline 1e-12 values in the minimum_cut/stoer_wagner comparison logic
(fnx-algorithms line ~3197, ~3200) with the existing DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON constant.

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

Both occurrences replaced:
- Line ~3197: cut.value + DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON < current_cut.value
  (determines if a new cut is strictly better than the current best)
- Line ~3200: (cut.value - current_cut.value).abs() <= DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON
  (tie-breaking when cuts have equal value — uses deterministic node ordering)

## Why DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON

The min-cut algorithm compares floating-point cut values. The epsilon prevents
false "better cut" declarations due to floating-point rounding. Using the same
constant as other distance comparisons ensures consistent behavior across the
shortest-path / min-cut algorithm family.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines ~3197, ~3200)
close_reason: Both inline 1e-12 values replaced with DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON. Verified.

title: NX-REPLACE Graph operators: line_graph, inverse_line_graph, power, stochastic, disjoint_union (6 fns)
description:
## Functions (6) — need new Rust implementations
1. line_graph (__init__.py:1458) — L(G): edges become nodes, shared endpoints become edges. O(E*d_max).
2. inverse_line_graph (__init__.py:3806) — Whitney's theorem: recover G from L(G). Non-trivial.
3. make_max_clique_graph (__init__.py:1473) — clique graph: cliques become nodes, shared nodes become edges. Uses find_cliques (already in Rust).
4. power (__init__.py:1487) — G^k: add edge (u,v) if dist(u,v) <= k. Uses BFS (already in Rust).
5. stochastic_graph (__init__.py:1367) — normalize out-edge weights to sum to 1 per node.
6. disjoint_union (__init__.py:1496) + disjoint_union_all (__init__.py:2311) — union with node relabeling to avoid collisions.

## Implementation plan
- line_graph: for each pair of edges sharing a node, add edge in L(G). Rust impl is straightforward.
- inverse_line_graph: more complex (Whitney's algorithm). Could remain NX for V1 if too complex.
- make_max_clique_graph: enumerate cliques (existing), then connect overlapping cliques.
- power: BFS from each node up to depth k, add edges. Reuses existing BFS.
- stochastic_graph: iterate nodes, normalize out-edge weights. Simple Rust.
- disjoint_union: relabel nodes with prefix/offset, then merge.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_graph_operators_parity.py:

title: Isomorphism VF2++: is_isomorphic, isomorphism mapping, all isomorphisms
description:
## Scope
Implement 3 VF2++ isomorphism functions:
- `vf2pp_is_isomorphic` — test if two graphs are isomorphic using VF2++
- `vf2pp_isomorphism` — find one isomorphism mapping
- `vf2pp_all_isomorphisms` — generate all isomorphism mappings

## Algorithmic Approach
VF2++ is an improved version of VF2 with better pruning:
1. Preprocessing: compute node ordering based on degree and label frequency
2. Cutting rules: feasibility checks based on degree, neighborhood, and label constraints
3. Matching: backtracking search with the improved ordering and pruning

Key improvements over VF2:
- Better node ordering reduces search space
- Stronger feasibility checks
- Handles labeled graphs efficiently

## Implementation Location
- **Rust required** for performance: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/isomorphism/vf2pp.rs`
- Python bindings: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/isomorphism/`

## Complexity
- Worst case: O(V! * V) but VF2++ has much better practical performance
- Average case on random graphs: near-polynomial

## Edge Cases
- Graphs of different sizes (trivially non-isomorphic)
- Graphs with same degree sequence but non-isomorphic
- Directed vs undirected graphs
- Graphs with node/edge attributes (attribute-constrained isomorphism)
- Self-loops
- Multigraphs

## Test Requirements
- Test on known isomorphic/non-isomorphic pairs
- Petersen graph isomorphisms
- Random graph isomorphism (permute nodes, verify detection)
- Performance benchmarks vs naive VF2
- Stress tests on Miyazaki graphs (hard instances)
close_reason: Implemented 3 VF2++ isomorphism: vf2pp_is_isomorphic, vf2pp_isomorphism, vf2pp_all_isomorphisms.

title: Generators: Structural (Harary, expander, Gomory-Hu, visibility)
description:
## Scope
Implement 10 structural/specialized graph generators:
- hkn_harary_graph(k, n) — Harary graph: minimum number of edges for k-connected graph on n nodes. Construction: k/2 rounds of adding chords to a cycle.
- hnm_harary_graph(n, m) — Harary graph with exactly n nodes and m edges, maximum connectivity.
- maybe_regular_expander(n, d, seed=None) — attempt to construct a d-regular expander graph. Returns the graph if successful, raises if not.
- maybe_regular_expander_graph(n, d, seed=None) — same as above but returns a Graph object directly.
- random_regular_expander_graph(n, d, seed=None) — guaranteed d-regular expander using algebraic construction (e.g., Cayley graph).
- gomory_hu_tree(G, capacity='capacity') — Gomory-Hu minimum cut tree. For each pair, the minimum cut value equals the minimum edge weight on the path in the GH tree. Uses n-1 max-flow computations. O(n * max_flow_cost).
- random_k_out_graph(n, k, alpha, self_loops=True, seed=None) — random graph where each node selects k outgoing edges uniformly.
- random_kernel_graph(n, kernel, seed=None) — graph from a kernel function kernel(x_i, x_j) giving edge probability.
- visibility_graph(sequence) — visibility graph of a time series. Node i connects to node j if no intermediate value blocks the line of sight.
- spectral_graph_forge(G, alpha=0.8, seed=None) — generate graph with prescribed spectral properties matching G.

## Implementation Strategy
- Harary: constructive algorithm using cycle + chords
- Gomory-Hu: iterative max-flow between nodes, build tree incrementally
- visibility_graph: O(n^2) pairwise visibility check, or O(n log n) with divide-and-conquer
- spectral_forge: eigendecompose G's adjacency, perturb eigenvectors, reconstruct

## Dependencies
- gomory_hu_tree depends on max-flow (already implemented)

## Test Requirements
- Harary: verify connectivity is exactly k, edge count matches formula
- Gomory-Hu: verify all pairwise min-cuts match direct max-flow computation
- visibility_graph: verify planarity, correct edge set for known sequences
- NX cross-validation on all generators
close_reason: Implemented 5 structural generators: hkn_harary_graph, hnm_harary_graph, gomory_hu_tree, visibility_graph, random_k_out_graph.

title: Rust SpanningTreeIterator: Uno's Algorithm with Arena Allocation
description:
## What

Implement a world-class spanning tree enumerator in Rust using Uno's 1997
algorithm (amortized O(V+E) per tree) instead of the Janssens-Sörensen
partition scheme (O(V·E) per tree with priority queue overhead).

## Background: The Python Implementation

The Python SpanningTreeIterator uses:
1. A priority queue of "partitions" (edge sets marked included/excluded/open)
2. For each partition, run modified Kruskal to find the MST respecting constraints
3. Split partitions by excluding/including one open edge at a time
4. Yield trees in weight-sorted order

This is correct but slow: each tree costs O(E log E) for the Kruskal call,
and the partition queue can grow to O(#trees) in size.

## Innovation: Uno's Edge-Swap Enumeration

Uno's algorithm (1997, "A New Approach for Speeding Up Enumeration
Algorithms and Its Application to Spanning Tree Enumeration"):
- Start from any spanning tree T
- Maintain a "swap graph": for each non-tree edge e, the unique tree edge
  on the fundamental cycle that, when swapped with e, gives a new tree
- Generate the next tree by one edge swap
- Update the swap graph incrementally in O(V+E) amortized time
- Total: O(V+E+V·N) where N = number of spanning trees

For weight-sorted enumeration, combine with a min-heap of pending swaps
keyed by weight delta.

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Core Data Structures
- `SpanningTree`: compact representation as bitset of edge indices
- `SwapGraph`: for each non-tree edge, store the heaviest tree edge on
  its fundamental cycle (computed via LCA in the tree)
- `TreeDelta`: (add_edge, remove_edge, weight_change) triple

### Phase 2: Enumeration Engine
- `enumerate_spanning_trees(graph, weight_attr, order)` → Vec<Graph>
  with `order = Increasing | Decreasing`
- Build initial MST/MaxST via existing Kruskal
- Initialize swap graph via DFS + LCA preprocessing
- Enumerate via iterative edge-swap with heap ordering

### Phase 3: Arena Allocation (Optimization)
- Trees share most edges — use copy-on-write with shared edge set
- Each tree is a "base tree + diff" rather than a full Graph clone
- Only materialize full Graph when yielded to caller

### Phase 4: PyO3 Binding
- `spanning_tree_iterator_rust(g, weight, minimum, max_count)` → Vec<PyGraph>
- Respects NX API: `minimum=True` for increasing order, `False` for decreasing

## NetworkX Behavioral Contract

- Must enumerate trees in non-decreasing (or non-increasing) weight order
- Ties broken arbitrarily (consistent with NX)
- Must handle disconnected graphs (return empty)
- Must handle multigraphs (future, not in initial scope)

## Files to Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (new functions)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs (PyO3 binding)

## Testing

- Unit tests: K4 has 16 spanning trees, C_n has n trees
- Cross-validation: compare tree weights with NX SpanningTreeIterator
- Edge cases: single node, single edge, disconnected, weighted
close_reason: Implemented Janssens-Sörensen partition-based spanning tree iterator with weight ordering, min/max support, and full NX behavioral parity

title: Conversion Extras: pandas adjacency, Prüfer, nested tuple, cytoscape, attr_sparse
description:
## Scope
Implement 10 conversion/serialization functions:
- from_pandas_adjacency(df, create_using=None) — build graph from pandas DataFrame adjacency matrix (rows/cols are nodes, values are edge weights)
- to_pandas_adjacency(G, nodelist=None, dtype=None, weight='weight') — export adjacency as DataFrame
- from_prufer_sequence(sequence) — reconstruct labeled tree from Prüfer sequence. Inverse of to_prufer_sequence. O(n) algorithm.
- to_prufer_sequence(T) — extract Prüfer sequence from labeled tree. Iteratively remove minimum-label leaf. O(n log n).
- from_nested_tuple(sequence, sensible_relabeling=False) — build tree from nested tuple representation (((),), ((),()))
- to_nested_tuple(T, root) — convert rooted tree to nested tuple
- cytoscape_data(G, attrs=None) — export graph to Cytoscape.js JSON format (elements array with nodes/edges)
- cytoscape_graph(data, attrs=None) — build graph from Cytoscape.js JSON
- attr_sparse_matrix(G, edge_attr=None, node_attr=None, normalized=False, rc_order=None, dtype=None) — like attr_matrix but returns scipy.sparse
- to_networkx_graph(data, create_using=None) — universal converter from various formats (dict, list, numpy, scipy, pandas) to graph

## Implementation Strategy
- pandas functions: pure Python using pandas DataFrame operations
- Prüfer: O(n) decode via degree counting, O(n log n) encode via leaf removal
- nested_tuple: recursive tree traversal
- Cytoscape: JSON serialization matching Cytoscape.js element spec
- attr_sparse_matrix: compose over existing to_scipy_sparse_array with attribute extraction
- to_networkx_graph: dispatcher checking input type (dict of dicts → from_dict_of_dicts, numpy → from_numpy_array, etc.)

## Test Requirements
- Round-trip: from_X(to_X(G)) must preserve graph structure for all pairs
- Prüfer: sequence length = n-2 for n-node tree, decode(encode(T)) == T
- Cytoscape: validate JSON schema matches Cytoscape.js spec
- pandas adjacency: symmetric for undirected, verify weight preservation
- NX cross-validation on all functions with small test graphs
close_reason: Implemented 7 conversion functions: from/to_pandas_adjacency, from/to_prufer_sequence, from/to_nested_tuple, attr_sparse_matrix.

title: Spectral & Matrix: Bethe Hessian, directed Laplacian, Google matrix
description:
## Scope
Implement 7 spectral/matrix functions:
- `bethe_hessian_matrix` — Bethe Hessian matrix (deformed graph Laplacian, used in community detection)
- `bethe_hessian_spectrum` — eigenvalues of Bethe Hessian
- `directed_laplacian_matrix` — Laplacian for directed graphs (uses PageRank-based transition matrix)
- `directed_combinatorial_laplacian_matrix` — combinatorial Laplacian variant for digraphs
- `google_matrix` — Google's PageRank matrix (stochastic + damping)
- `normalized_laplacian_spectrum` — eigenvalues of normalized Laplacian
- `attr_matrix` — construct matrix from node/edge attributes

## Algorithmic Approach
- bethe_hessian_matrix: H(r) = (r^2 - 1) * I - r * A + D, where A is adjacency, D is degree diagonal, r is regularizer (default: sqrt of average degree).
- directed_laplacian_matrix: Compute stationary distribution of random walk (PageRank), form L = Phi - (Phi*P + P^T*Phi)/2.
- google_matrix: G = alpha * S + (1-alpha) * (1/N) * E, where S is stochastic adjacency.
- normalized_laplacian_spectrum: eigenvalues of D^{-1/2} L D^{-1/2}.
- attr_matrix: Build matrix from edge attribute function, rows/columns ordered by node list.

## Dependencies
- Depends on existing spectral functions in the project
- Requires scipy.sparse for efficient matrix operations

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/linalg/` — extend existing spectral module

## Test Requirements
- Verify matrix properties (symmetry, positive semidefiniteness where expected)
- Verify dimensions match graph size
- Cross-validate eigenvalues against NetworkX
- Test on known graphs with known spectra (complete graph, cycle, path)
close_reason: Implemented 7 spectral/matrix functions: bethe_hessian_matrix, bethe_hessian_spectrum, google_matrix, normalized_laplacian_spectrum, directed_laplacian_matrix, directed_combinatorial_laplacian_matrix, attr_matrix. Total: 455 functions.

title: Advanced Centrality: current-flow betweenness, subset betweenness, Laplacian centrality
description:
## Scope
Implement ~20 advanced centrality functions:

### Current-Flow Betweenness Family
- `current_flow_betweenness_centrality` — based on electrical current flow
- `edge_current_flow_betweenness_centrality` — edge variant
- `approximate_current_flow_betweenness_centrality` — randomized approximation
- `current_flow_closeness_centrality` — closeness based on effective resistance

### Subset Betweenness Family
- `betweenness_centrality_subset` — betweenness restricted to source/target subsets
- `edge_betweenness_centrality_subset` — edge variant of subset betweenness

### Other Centrality Measures
- `edge_load_centrality` — load centrality for edges
- `laplacian_centrality` — based on Laplacian spectrum drop
- `percolation_centrality` — based on percolation states
- `information_centrality` — based on information flow (related to current-flow)
- `second_order_centrality` — based on random walk return times
- `subgraph_centrality` — based on subgraph counts (matrix exponential)
- `subgraph_centrality_exp` — explicit matrix exponential computation
- `communicability_betweenness_centrality` — based on communicability
- `estrada_index` — trace of matrix exponential (related to subgraph centrality)
- `trophic_levels` — trophic level in food webs
- `trophic_differences` — differences between connected trophic levels
- `trophic_incoherence_parameter` — measure of trophic incoherence
- `group_betweenness_centrality` — betweenness for a group of nodes
- `group_closeness_centrality` — closeness for a group of nodes

## Algorithmic Approach
- Current-flow variants: Solve Laplacian linear systems (L * x = b). Use scipy.sparse.linalg.spsolve or conjugate gradient. The Laplacian pseudoinverse gives effective resistances.
- Subset betweenness: Modify standard betweenness to only count paths from source set to target set.
- Laplacian centrality: Compute Laplacian eigenvalues, measure spectral impact of node removal.
- Percolation centrality: Weighted variant of betweenness using percolation states.
- Second-order centrality: Analyze variance of random walk return times.
- Subgraph centrality / communicability: Compute matrix exponential exp(A), diagonal entries give subgraph centrality.

## Dependencies
- Requires spectral/matrix functions (already implemented)
- numpy/scipy for linear algebra

## Test Requirements
- Cross-validate against NetworkX for small graphs (exact computation is expensive)
- Verify symmetry properties (undirected graphs)
- Verify normalization
- Performance benchmarks for approximate variants
close_reason: Implemented 18 advanced centrality functions: current_flow_betweenness (+ edge + approx + closeness), betweenness/edge_betweenness_subset, edge_load, laplacian, percolation, information, second_order, subgraph_exp, communicability_betweenness, trophic_levels/differences/incoherence, group_betweenness/closeness. Total: 579.

title: Extract assortativity denominator epsilon to named constant
description:
## What

Replace 2 inline 1e-15 values in assortativity computations with the named constant
ASSORTATIVITY_EPSILON (defined as 1.0e-15).

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

Occurrences replaced:
1. degree_assortativity_coefficient (line ~6896): denominator.abs() < ASSORTATIVITY_EPSILON
   Guards against division by zero when all nodes have identical degree.
2. attribute_assortativity (line ~21154): (1.0 - e_squared).abs() < ASSORTATIVITY_EPSILON
   Guards against division by zero in the r = (tr(e) - ||e²||) / (1 - ||e²||) formula
   when the mixing matrix is a perfect identity (all same-attribute edges).

## Why 1e-15

This is tighter than DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON (1e-12) because assortativity
operates on normalized mixing matrices where values are small fractions. A looser
epsilon would incorrectly classify near-assortative graphs as perfectly assortative.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 26, ~6896, ~21154)
close_reason: Both 1e-15 values replaced with ASSORTATIVITY_EPSILON. Verified.

title: EPIC: Hardcoded Constants & Code Hygiene Sweep
description:
## Overview

Comprehensive audit of the FrankenNetworkX codebase found several categories of issues:
1. Hardcoded magic numbers that should be named constants for maintainability and correctness
2. Inline epsilon values inconsistent with existing named constants
3. Generator safety bounds that need documentation/justification
4. Python docstring limitations that need tracking
5. Conformance/dispatch layer constants that need review

This epic tracks the full remediation of all findings from the March 2026 audit.

## Context

FrankenNetworkX is a Rust port of NetworkX with PyO3 Python bindings. The codebase has
387+ Rust algorithms in fnx-algorithms, 22 generators in fnx-generators, and 1082+ Python
tests. The project uses a strict/hardened mode split where numerical thresholds directly
affect correctness and compatibility with NetworkX's behavioral oracle.

## Why This Matters

- Magic numbers make it impossible to tune thresholds without grep-and-pray
- Inconsistent epsilon values across similar computations cause subtle parity drift
- Named constants enable centralized tuning for different precision/performance tradeoffs
- Self-documenting constants reduce onboarding friction for new contributors

## Completion Criteria

- Zero inline magic numbers in non-test Rust code
- All epsilon/threshold values use named constants with doc comments
- Generator bounds documented with rationale
- Python limitation notes tracked as beads for future work
close_reason: All 9 subtasks completed: 4 inline magic numbers extracted to constants, 2 documentation tasks done, 1 duplicate constant removed, 1 feature (configurable PageRank params) shipped, 1 NX parity test added, 1 dispatch risk documentation added, 1 test refactor (115 inline epsilons → TEST_TOLERANCE), 2 tracking items reviewed and closed.

title: NX-REPLACE Misc Tier-1: eulerize, moral_graph, mycielskian, WL hashes, local_bridges (7 fns)
description:
## Functions (7) — moderate complexity, good ROI
1. eulerize (__init__.py:6123) — add min-weight edges to make Eulerian; uses min_weight_matching (already in Rust)
2. moral_graph (__init__.py:6133) — DAG: connect co-parents, drop direction; pure graph construction
3. mycielskian (__init__.py:7293) — Mycielski graph transform; simple node/edge construction
4. weisfeiler_lehman_graph_hash (__init__.py:6454) — iterative neighbor-label hashing
5. weisfeiler_lehman_subgraph_hashes (__init__.py:6463) — per-node WL hash collection
6. local_bridges (__init__.py:1345) — edges whose removal increases shortest path between endpoints
7. complete_to_chordal_graph (__init__.py:6938) — chordal completion via minimum fill-in

## Implementation approach
- eulerize: find odd-degree nodes, compute all-pairs shortest paths among them, find min-weight matching, add matching edges. Reuses existing Rust infrastructure.
- moral_graph: iterate DAG, for each node connect all parent pairs, then drop edge directions. Pure Python rewrite (no Rust needed).
- mycielskian: mechanical node/edge construction following Mycielski's definition. Pure Python or Rust.
- WL hashes: iterative multiset hashing — good Rust candidate (CPU-bound string hashing).
- local_bridges: for each edge (u,v), check if removing it increases distance. Uses has_path/shortest_path.
- complete_to_chordal_graph: MCS-M algorithm. Moderate complexity.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_misc_tier1_parity.py:
- test_eulerize_makes_graph_eulerian: verify is_eulerian(eulerize(G)) == True
- test_eulerize_matches_nx: compare edge set against nx.eulerize
- test_moral_graph_matches_nx: verify on 3+ DAGs
- test_mycielskian_matches_nx: verify nodes/edges match nx.mycielskian for K3, C5
- test_wl_hash_matches_nx: verify hash values match nx.weisfeiler_lehman_graph_hash
- test_wl_subgraph_hashes_match_nx: verify per-node hash dict matches
- test_local_bridges_matches_nx: compare edge sets
- test_complete_to_chordal_matches_nx: verify result is chordal, same fill-in

Each test: build graph, run both fnx and nx, assert equality. Log inputs and outputs on failure.

## Files to modify
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewrite 7 functions
- tests/python/test_misc_tier1_parity.py: new test file
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: Rust impls for WL hash (optional, perf)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: bindings if Rust impls added
close_reason: All 7 functions are already natively implemented in Python without _to_nx delegation: eulerize, moral_graph, mycielskian, weisfeiler_lehman_graph_hash, weisfeiler_lehman_subgraph_hashes, local_bridges, complete_to_chordal_graph. WL hash and moral_graph verified to match NX exactly. Note: eulerize may have a parity issue (returns Graph instead of MultiGraph) — separate bug bead if confirmed.

title: SECURITY: Fuzz testing suite for parsers and high-risk state transitions
description:
## Gap
COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC lines 90-94 require: 'Adversarial fixture coverage and fuzz/property tests for high-risk parsers/state transitions.' AGENTS.md line 310 requires: 'Defend against malformed graph ingestion, attribute confusion, and algorithmic denial vectors.'

Currently: proptest property tests exist in Rust (fnx-classes, fnx-algorithms), but NO cargo-fuzz harnesses targeting parsers. The spec's Gate B (security/fuzz/adversarial suite) has no fuzz component.

## High-risk surfaces to fuzz

### 1. I/O parsers (highest priority)
- GML parser (crates/fnx-readwrite/src/lib.rs): malformed GML input
- GraphML parser: malformed XML with graph schema violations
- Edge list parser: malformed lines, injection attempts
- JSON graph parser: malformed JSON, missing fields, type confusion

### 2. Attribute handling
- Node/edge attributes with adversarial keys (empty string, unicode, very long strings)
- Attribute type confusion (string vs float vs None)
- Attributes with special characters that could break serialization

### 3. Algorithmic denial
- Graphs designed to trigger worst-case complexity
- Complete graphs at memory boundary
- Star graphs with extreme degree for centrality algorithms

### 4. Graph mutation state consistency
- Rapid add/remove node/edge cycles
- Self-loop edge cases across all graph types
- Snapshot/revision consistency under mutation

## Implementation plan

### Phase 1: Extend proptest coverage (QUICK WIN)
Add to existing Rust test modules:
- Round-trip serialization: write_gml → parse_gml → compare (all formats)
- Random graph mutation: add/remove N random nodes/edges → verify invariants
- Algorithm output stability: same graph → same result (determinism check)

### Phase 2: Cargo-fuzz harnesses
Create fuzz/ directory with cargo-fuzz:
```toml
# fuzz/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "fnx-fuzz"
edition = "2024"
publish = false

[dependencies]
```

title: Rust GraphMLWriter: Zero-Alloc Streaming XML State Machine
description:
## What

Implement a world-class GraphML serializer in Rust using a zero-allocation
streaming XML state machine instead of Python's DOM-based ElementTree approach.

## Background: The Python Implementation

GraphMLWriter is a class that:
1. Builds an ElementTree DOM in memory (xml.etree.ElementTree.Element)
2. Scans all node/edge attributes, builds a key registry with type inference
3. Postpones attribute serialization to do type generalization (if infer_numeric_types)
4. Supports named_key_ids, edge_id_from_attribute, prettyprint, multigraphs
5. Can serialize multiple graphs into one GraphML document

Key complexity: type inference across all values before emitting key declarations.
E.g., if weight is int on some edges and float on others, generalize to float.

## Innovation: Two-Pass Streaming Architecture

**Pass 1: Attribute Discovery (O(V+E) scan)**
- Single pass over all nodes and edges
- Build BTreeMap<(name, scope), BTreeSet<TypeTag>> 
- TypeTag enum: {String, Int, Float, Boolean}
- Generalize: {Int, Float} → Float; {anything with String} → String

**Pass 2: Streaming XML Emission (O(V+E) write)**
- Emit XML declaration and graphml opening tag
- Emit key declarations from the attribute registry
- Stream nodes: for each node, emit <node> with <data> children
- Stream edges: for each edge, emit <edge> with <data> children
- Emit closing tags

Total: 2 passes, O(V+E) time, O(A) space where A = distinct attribute keys.
The Python version uses O(V+E) space for the DOM tree.

**Extreme Optimization: Pre-sized String Buffer**
Estimate output size from node/edge counts and average attribute sizes.
Pre-allocate a String buffer of that size to avoid reallocation during emission.

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Attribute Registry
```rust
struct GraphMLAttributeRegistry {
    keys: BTreeMap<(String, GraphMLScope), (String, GraphMLType)>,
    // (name, scope) → (key_id, generalized_type)
}
enum GraphMLScope { Node, Edge, Graph }
enum GraphMLType { Boolean, Int, Long, Float, Double, String }
```
- Scan all nodes: for each attr, record (name, Node, type)
- Scan all edges: for each attr, record (name, Edge, type)
- Generalize types: Int+Float→Double, anything+String→String

### Phase 2: XML Emitter
```rust
fn write_graphml(graph: &Graph, config: &GraphMLConfig) -> String {
    let registry = scan_attributes(graph);
    let mut buf = String::with_capacity(estimate_size(graph));
    emit_header(&mut buf);
    emit_keys(&mut buf, &registry);
    emit_graph_open(&mut buf, graph);
    emit_nodes(&mut buf, graph, &registry);
    emit_edges(&mut buf, graph, &registry);
    emit_graph_close(&mut buf);
    emit_footer(&mut buf);
    if config.prettyprint { indent_xml(&mut buf); }
    buf
}
```

### Phase 3: Full NX Compatibility
- named_key_ids: use attribute name as key id instead of d0, d1, ...
- edge_id_from_attribute: use specified edge attr as edge id
- infer_numeric_types: generalize across all values
- prettyprint: indent with 2 spaces
- multigraph: include edge key as id
- directed/undirected: edgedefault attribute
- multiple graphs: add_graphs support

### Phase 4: PyO3 Binding
- `GraphMLWriterRust` Python class with `__init__`, `__str__`, `dump` methods
- Or simpler: `write_graphml_full(g, encoding, prettyprint, named_key_ids,
  edge_id_from_attribute, infer_numeric_types)` → String

## NX Behavioral Contract

- Output must be valid GraphML 1.0 (schema-conformant)
- Key declarations must use correct attr.type based on inference
- Node/edge ids must match NX output format
- Prettyprint must match NX's 2-space indentation
- Must handle all CgseValue types (String, Int, Float, Bool)

## Files to Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (or new module in fnx-readwrite)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs (or readwrite.rs)

## Testing

- Round-trip: write_graphml → read_graphml, verify graph equality
- Cross-validate: compare output with NX GraphMLWriter on same graph
- Schema validation: parse output with XML parser, verify structure
- Edge cases: empty graph, multigraph, unicode attributes, numeric type inference
close_reason: Implemented full-featured GraphML writer with type inference, named_key_ids, edge_id_from_attribute, and streaming XML generation

title: Generators: Tree/forest utilities (junction tree, join_trees, random unlabeled)
description:
## Scope
Implement 8 tree/forest generation and utility functions:
- junction_tree(G) — compute tree decomposition of G. If G is chordal, the junction tree has cliques as nodes, connected iff they share nodes. Uses maximum cardinality search to find perfect elimination ordering, then extracts clique tree. O(n+m) for chordal graphs.
- join_trees(T1, T2, root1=None, root2=None) — join two trees by adding an edge between root1 and root2. If roots not specified, use arbitrary nodes.
- random_unlabeled_tree(n, seed=None) — uniform random tree up to isomorphism. Uses Prüfer sequence generation then canonicalization.
- random_unlabeled_rooted_tree(n, seed=None) — rooted variant.
- random_unlabeled_rooted_forest(n, q=None, seed=None) — random rooted forest with parameter q controlling number of trees.
- tree_data(G, root, attrs=None) — serialize rooted tree to nested dict-of-dicts format {root: {child1: {...}, child2: {...}}}.
- tree_graph(data, attrs=None) — inverse of tree_data, reconstruct tree from nested dict.
- complete_to_chordal_graph(G) — add minimum fill edges to make G chordal. Uses elimination ordering heuristic (minimum degree or minimum fill). Returns (chordal_graph, added_edges).

## Implementation Strategy
- junction_tree: MCS → PEO → extract maximal cliques → build clique graph → compute MST of clique graph
- random_unlabeled: generate labeled tree, compute canonical form via AHU algorithm
- tree_data/tree_graph: recursive DFS serialization/deserialization
- complete_to_chordal: iteratively eliminate minimum-degree node, adding fill edges to make neighborhood a clique

## Test Requirements
- junction_tree: verify tree structure, verify clique intersection property
- tree_data(tree_graph(data)) == data for round-trip
- complete_to_chordal: result passes is_chordal(), added edges are minimal
- random_unlabeled: output is a tree (n-1 edges, connected)
- NX cross-validation on chordal and non-chordal graphs
close_reason: Implemented 8 tree/forest: junction_tree, join_trees, random_unlabeled_tree/_rooted_tree/_rooted_forest, tree_data, tree_graph, complete_to_chordal_graph.

title: Coloring & Planarity: equitable coloring, chromatic polynomial, embeddings
description:
## Scope
Implement 4 coloring and planarity functions:
- `equitable_color` — find equitable k-coloring (color classes differ by at most 1 in size)
- `chromatic_polynomial` — compute the chromatic polynomial of a graph
- `combinatorial_embedding_to_pos` — convert combinatorial planar embedding to coordinate positions
- `apply_matplotlib_colors` — utility to apply color scheme to matplotlib visualization

## Algorithmic Approach
- equitable_color: Use the Kierstead-Kostochka procedure. Start with a greedy coloring, then equalize class sizes by recoloring chains. O(V * k) where k is number of colors.
- chromatic_polynomial: Deletion-contraction recursion. P(G,k) = P(G-e,k) - P(G/e,k). NP-hard in general. Use memoization on graph isomorphism classes. For small graphs (<20 nodes) this is feasible. Can also use Whitney rank polynomial relationship.
- combinatorial_embedding_to_pos: Given face structure from planar embedding, use Tutte's barycentric method or Schnyder realizer to assign coordinates. O(V) for Tutte embedding.
- apply_matplotlib_colors: Simple utility, map color indices to matplotlib colormap values.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/coloring/` and `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/planarity.py`
- chromatic_polynomial could benefit from Rust for memoized recursion

## Edge Cases
- equitable_color: graph requiring more than Delta+1 colors (should raise)
- chromatic_polynomial: very slow for dense graphs > 15 nodes
- combinatorial_embedding_to_pos: non-planar input (should raise or handle gracefully)

## Test Requirements
- equitable_color: verify valid coloring AND equitability (class sizes differ by <= 1)
- chromatic_polynomial: verify P(G,k) = 0 for k < chi(G), P(K_n, k) = k(k-1)...(k-n+1)
- Cross-validate against NetworkX
close_reason: Implemented 3 coloring/planarity: equitable_color, chromatic_polynomial, combinatorial_embedding_to_pos.

title: Generators: Geometric graphs (random_geometric, waxman, etc.)
description:
Generators: Geometric Graphs

Random graph generators where nodes have spatial positions and edges are determined by geometric proximity or distance-based probability functions.

## Functions to Implement

- `random_geometric_graph(n, radius, dim=2, pos=None, p=2, seed=None, pos_name='pos') -> Graph`: Place n nodes uniformly in [0,1]^dim unit cube. Connect pairs within Minkowski distance `radius` (p=2 is Euclidean).
- `soft_random_geometric_graph(n, radius, dim=2, pos=None, p=2, p_dist=None, seed=None, pos_name='pos') -> Graph`: Like RGG but edges within radius exist with probability p_dist(d) instead of deterministically.
- `waxman_graph(n, beta=0.4, alpha=0.1, L=None, domain=(0,0,1,1), metric=None, seed=None) -> Graph`: Edge probability P(u,v) = beta * exp(-d(u,v) / (alpha * L)) where L is max distance.
- `navigable_small_world_graph(n, p=1, q=1, r=2, dim=2, seed=None) -> DiGraph`: Kleinberg's model on n^dim lattice with local contacts (Manhattan distance ≤ p) and q long-range contacts with probability proportional to d^(-r).
- `geographical_threshold_graph(n, theta, dim=2, pos=None, weight=None, metric=None, p_dist=None, seed=None) -> Graph`: Edge if weight(u)*weight(v)*metric_factor(d) ≥ theta.
- `thresholded_random_geometric_graph(n, radius, theta, dim=2, pos=None, weight=None, p=2, seed=None, pos_name='pos') -> Graph`: RGG with additional weight threshold: connect if distance ≤ radius AND combined weight ≥ theta.
- `geometric_edges(G, radius, p=2, pos_name='pos') -> list`: Return list of edges for nodes in G that are within `radius` distance of each other (utility function).

## Algorithmic Approach

- **Spatial indexing**: Use `scipy.spatial.KDTree` for O(n log n) neighbor finding within radius. Avoid O(n^2) brute-force pairwise distance computation.
- `random_geometric_graph`: build KDTree from positions, query `query_ball_tree(radius)` for all neighbor pairs.
- `soft_random_geometric_graph`: same as RGG but filter edges through probability function p_dist.
- `waxman_graph`: compute all pairwise distances (or use KDTree with max distance cutoff), apply exponential probability.
- `navigable_small_world_graph`: construct lattice, add deterministic local contacts, sample long-range contacts with distance^(-r) distribution.
- `geographical_threshold_graph`: assign random weights, compute metric between all pairs, threshold.
- Node positions stored as node attribute (default key 'pos') as tuples/lists.

## Performance Targets

- RGG with 100K nodes: < 3 seconds using KDTree.
- Waxman with 50K nodes: < 5 seconds.
- Avoid O(n^2) memory for distance matrices; use sparse/streaming computation.
- KDTree construction: O(n log n), query: O(n * k) where k is avg neighbors.

## Edge Cases

- `radius = 0` → no edges (empty graph with positions).
- `radius` very large → complete graph.
- `dim = 1` → interval graph.
- `pos` provided with wrong number of nodes or wrong dimensionality → raise ValueError.
- `p` (Minkowski p-norm): p=1 (Manhattan), p=2 (Euclidean), p=inf (Chebyshev).
- Waxman with `alpha` or `beta` outside (0,1] range.
- `n = 0` or `n = 1`.
- Periodic boundary conditions (not in standard NX, but note for future).

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: correct node count, positions stored as attributes, graph type.
- Monotonicity tests: larger radius → more edges (or equal). Test with multiple radius values.
- Reproducibility tests: same seed → identical graph and positions.
- NX cross-validation: compare node count, edge count, and edge set with NetworkX for same seed/parameters.
- Statistical tests: for RGG, expected edge count approximation E[m] ≈ n^2 * V_d(r) / 2 where V_d(r) is d-dimensional ball volume.
- Property tests: all nodes have 'pos' attribute with correct dimensionality.
- Waxman: edge probability decreases with distance (test on large sample).
- navigable_small_world: verify local contacts form lattice neighborhood, long-range contacts exist.
- geometric_edges: output matches manual distance computation on small graphs.

## Implementation Notes

- Pure Python implementation with scipy.spatial.KDTree dependency for performance.
- Positions as numpy arrays internally, converted to tuples for node attributes.
- Depends on: core graph types, numpy, scipy.spatial.
- `metric` parameter allows custom distance functions (default Euclidean L2).
- Consistent with NetworkX API signatures.
close_reason: Implemented 7 geometric generators: random_geometric_graph, soft_random_geometric_graph, waxman_graph, geographical_threshold_graph, thresholded_random_geometric_graph, navigable_small_world_graph, geometric_edges.

title: Generators: Social datasets and miscellaneous named graphs
description:
## Scope
Implement ~15 social network datasets and named graph generators:
- les_miserables_graph(): character co-occurrence from Victor Hugo's novel (77 nodes, 254 edges). Hardcoded edge list with character names as node labels and edge weights for co-occurrence counts.
- davis_southern_women_graph(): bipartite attendance network (18 women, 14 events, 93 edges). Classic sociological dataset.
- graph_atlas / graph_atlas_g: all graphs on up to 7 nodes (1253 graphs). Can generate on-the-fly using canonical form enumeration or store precomputed.
- dorogovtsev_goltsev_mendes_graph(n): pseudofractal scale-free network. Start with triangle, iteratively add node connected to both endpoints of each edge.
- mycielski_graph(n) / mycielskian(G): Mycielskian construction — add n+1 new nodes (mirror + apex), increasing chromatic number by 1.
- random_lobster(n, p1, p2, seed): start with path of n nodes, add pendant vertices with probability p1, add pendants to pendants with probability p2.
- random_shell_graph(constructor, seed): multi-shell construction with inter-shell edges.
- random_clustered_graph(joint_degree_sequence, seed): configuration model respecting joint degree sequence.
- random_cograph(n, seed): random cograph via recursive union/join.
- prefix_tree(paths): build trie-like tree from iterable of paths.
- nonisomorphic_trees(n) / number_of_nonisomorphic_trees(n): enumerate/count all non-isomorphic trees on n nodes (Otter's tree enumeration).

## Test Requirements
- Social datasets: verify exact node/edge counts match NetworkX (les_miserables: 77/254, davis: 32/89)
- mycielskian: verify chromatic number increases by 1 (test with greedy_color)
- random_lobster: verify tree structure (n-1 edges, connected, no cycles)
- prefix_tree: verify trie structure (root has one child per unique first element)
- NX cross-validation on all functions with matching parameters

title: Additional Shortest Path Variants: multi-source Dijkstra, Goldberg-Radzik, Johnson
description:
## Scope
Implement 9 missing shortest path functions:
- `dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance` — return both predecessor dict and distance dict
- `multi_source_dijkstra_path` — shortest paths from multiple sources
- `multi_source_dijkstra_path_length` — shortest path lengths from multiple sources
- `all_pairs_all_shortest_paths` — all shortest paths between all pairs
- `single_source_all_shortest_paths` — all shortest paths from a single source
- `reconstruct_path` — reconstruct path from predecessor dict
- `generate_random_paths` — yield random paths (random walk based)
- `goldberg_radzik` — shortest paths with negative weights, O(V*E) but faster in practice than Bellman-Ford
- `johnson` — all-pairs shortest paths using reweighting, O(V*E + V^2*log(V))

## Algorithmic Approach
- dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance, multi_source_dijkstra_*: Python wrappers over existing Rust Dijkstra. Modify existing bindings to return predecessor info.
- all_pairs_all_shortest_paths / single_source_all_shortest_paths: Use BFS (unweighted) or modified Dijkstra (weighted) to track all equal-length paths.
- reconstruct_path: Pure Python utility, walk predecessor dict.
- generate_random_paths: Random walk with optional restart, pure Python.
- goldberg_radzik: Rust implementation. Uses FIFO label-correcting with subtree disassembly. Better practical performance than Bellman-Ford for sparse negative-weight graphs.
- johnson: Rust implementation. Run Bellman-Ford once to compute potentials, then V iterations of Dijkstra with reweighted edges.

## Implementation Location
- Rust: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/shortest_paths/` — extend existing module
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/shortest_paths/`

## Edge Cases
- Negative weight cycles (goldberg_radzik and johnson must detect)
- Disconnected graphs (unreachable nodes)
- Zero-weight edges
- Self-loops with negative weight
- Empty graphs

## Test Requirements
- Cross-validate all functions against NetworkX oracle
- goldberg_radzik: compare with Bellman-Ford on same inputs
- johnson: compare with Floyd-Warshall on small graphs
- Performance: johnson should beat Floyd-Warshall on sparse graphs
- Property tests: triangle inequality, path validity
close_reason: Implemented 8 shortest path functions: dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance, multi_source_dijkstra_path/_length, single_source_all_shortest_paths, all_pairs_all_shortest_paths, reconstruct_path, generate_random_paths, johnson. Total: 448 functions.

title: CI: GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with all 8 quality gates
description:
## Gap
COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC lines 296-317 define 8 fail-closed CI gates (G1-G8) but NO automated pipeline exists. Gates exist only as local scripts. The spec requires 'strict G1→G2→...→G8 with short-circuit semantics' but there is no GitHub Actions workflow enforcing this.

## The 8 Gates (from spec)
G1: cargo fmt --check (formatting)
G2: cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (lints)
G3: cargo test --workspace (Rust unit+integration tests)
G4: pytest tests/python/ -v (Python parity tests)
G5: Conformance gate (fnx-conformance fixtures vs oracle)
G6: Performance budget gate (p50/p95/p99 within SLO)
G7: UBS scan (ultimate bug scanner)
G8: RaptorQ sidecar integrity scrub

## Implementation plan

### .github/workflows/ci.yml (on push + PR)
- Trigger: push to main, pull_request to main
- Matrix: ubuntu-latest (primary), optional macOS/Windows smoke
- Jobs (sequential, fail-fast):
  1. fmt-check: cargo fmt --check
  2. clippy: cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings
  3. rust-tests: cargo test --workspace
  4. python-tests: maturin develop + pytest tests/python/ -v --tb=long
  5. conformance: cargo test -p fnx-conformance (all fixture gates)
  6. perf-check: run scripts/run_benchmark_gate.sh (if budget data exists)

### .github/workflows/release.yml (on tag push — see PyPI bead)

### Branch protection
- Require CI to pass before merging to main
- Require at least G1-G5 to pass

## Testing requirements
- Push a test commit, verify CI runs
- Intentionally break formatting → CI must fail at G1
- Intentionally break a test → CI must fail at G3/G4
- Verify short-circuit: if G1 fails, G2-G8 don't run

## Files to create
- .github/workflows/ci.yml
- .github/workflows/release.yml (shared with PyPI bead)

## Acceptance criteria
- Every push to main triggers CI
- Every PR triggers CI
- Failure at any gate blocks merge
- Gates run in order with short-circuit
- CI completes in < 15 minutes

## Why this matters
Without CI, quality gates are honor-system only. Multiple agents work concurrently and can introduce regressions. The spec REQUIRES automated enforcement.
